data.content:
Now I have all the info. Let me create the `Directory.Packages.props` and strip versions from all `.csproj` files.

Resolved versions (highest wins):
- `Azure.Identity`: 1.13.2 (Api=1.13.2, Shared=1.11.4)
- `System.Text.Json`: 10.0.1 (Api/Shared/Tests=10.0.1, Worker=8.0.4)
- Others: single version each

arguments.file_text:
<Project>
  <PropertyGroup>
    <ManagePackageVersionsCentrally>true</ManagePackageVersionsCentrally>
  </PropertyGroup>
  <ItemGroup>
    <PackageVersion Include="Azure.Identity" Version="1.13.2" />
    <PackageVersion Include="Microsoft.Extensions.Hosting" Version="8.0.1" />
    <PackageVersion Include="OpenTelemetry.Extensions.Hosting" Version="1.15.0" />
    <PackageVersion Include="System.Text.Json" Version="10.0.1" />
    <PackageVersion Include="xunit" Version="2.9.3" />
  </ItemGroup>
</Project>

data.reasoningText:
Now I have the full picture. Let me collect all package references and resolve conflicts by picking the highest version:

| Package | Api | Worker | Shared | Tests | Highest |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| System.Text.Json | 10.0.1 | 8.0.4 | 10.0.1 | 10.0.1 | 10.0.1 |
| Azure.Identity | 1.13.2 | - | 1.11.4 | - | 1.13.2 |
| Microsoft.Extensions.Hosting | - | 8.0.1 | - | - | 8.0.1 |


| OpenTelemetry.Extensions.Hosting | - | 1.15.0 | - | - | 1.15.0 |
| xunit | - | - | - | 2.9.3 | 2.9.3 |

Now I need to create a central package management file at the solution root that enables centralized versioning and lists all the package versions, then strip out the version attributes from each project file's package references.

data.content:
Done. Here's what was changed:

**Created** `Directory.Packages.props` with 5 centralized package versions, resolving 2 conflicts by picking the highest version:
- `Azure.Identity` → **1.13.2** (was 1.11.4 in Shared)
- `System.Text.Json` → **10.0.1** (was 8.0.4 in Worker)

**Updated** all 4 `.csproj` files — removed `Version` attributes from every `<PackageReference>`.
